---
title: "Scientific Writing"
subtitle: "Guidelines and templates for your seminar paper"
code-links:
  - text: Template (English)
    icon: file-earmark-text
    href: ../templates/seminar-paper-en.qmd
  - text: Template (German)
    icon: file-earmark-text
    href: ../templates/seminar-paper-de.qmd
  - text: Template (LaTeX)
    icon: file-earmark-code
    href: ../templates/Vorlage.tex
  - text: Bibliography
    icon: journal-bookmark
    href: ../templates/references.bib
  - text: Typst Helper
    icon: gear
    href: ../templates/typst-show.typ
---

# Getting Started

We provide ready-to-use templates for your seminar paper. The recommended option is **Quarto with Typst**, which produces professional PDFs without a LaTeX installation. A traditional LaTeX template is also available.

| Template | Format | Requirements |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| `seminar-paper-en.qmd` | English, Quarto/Typst | Quarto installed |
| `seminar-paper-de.qmd` | German, Quarto/Typst | Quarto installed |
| `Vorlage.tex` | German, LaTeX | LaTeX distribution + Biber |

: Available templates {#tbl-templates}

Download the files using the buttons in the sidebar. For the Quarto templates, you need three files in the same folder: the `.qmd` template, `references.bib`, and `typst-show.typ`. For detailed setup instructions and how to edit the title page, see the [Quarto workshop](../workshops/quarto-academic-writing.qmd).

:::{.callout-tip}
Use `quarto preview seminar-paper-en.qmd` to open a live preview that refreshes automatically every time you save the file.
:::


# Structure of a Seminar Paper

A typical seminar paper or thesis in operations research follows a standard structure. Each section has a specific purpose and readers expect information in a specific order.

## Introduction

Structure your introduction as a **funnel** — start broad and narrow down:

1. **Broad context** — Why does this field matter? What is the practical relevance?
2. **Specific problem** — What is the concrete challenge you address?
3. **Research gap** — What is missing in the existing literature?
4. **Your contribution** — What does this paper do? State it clearly in one or two sentences.
5. **Paper outline** — Briefly describe the structure of the remaining sections.

:::{.callout-tip}
Write the introduction last, once you know your actual contribution. It should answer three questions: Why should the reader care? What gap exists? What is new here?
:::

## Literature Review

The literature review is not a list of summaries. It should **build an argument** for why your work is needed.

- **Group by theme or method**, not chronologically. For example, group exact methods together, then heuristics, then machine-learning approaches.
- **Compare and contrast** — don't just state what each paper does, but how they relate to each other and where they fall short.
- **Identify the gap** that your work fills. Every cited paper should connect to your research question.
- **End with a transition** to your own contribution: what remains unsolved?

Use `[@key]` for parenthetical citations — *(Author, Year)* — and `@key` for narrative citations — *Author (Year)*.

## Model

### Assumptions

Every model relies on simplifying assumptions. For each assumption:

- **Justify it** — why is it reasonable for your problem?
- **Acknowledge limitations** — what would change if the assumption were relaxed?
- **Order** from most fundamental to most specific.

### Formulation

- **Define all notation before using it.** Use a notation table (the templates include one).
- Follow consistent conventions: sets in calligraphic letters ($\mathcal{J}$), decision variables in uppercase ($X_j$), parameters in lowercase ($b_i$).
- **Explain every constraint in prose** — don't just list equations. For example: *"Constraint (2) ensures that each customer is visited exactly once."*
- Reference constraints by their labels when discussing them.

## Computational Study

A computational study must be **reproducible**. Always report:

- **Hardware and software**: CPU, RAM, solver name and version, programming language.
- **Instance sources**: where the test data comes from, or how you generated it.
- **Baselines**: compare against a benchmark, exact solution, or published results.
- **Metrics**: report both solution quality (optimality gap, objective value) and computation time.
- **Trends**: discuss how performance changes across instance sizes — don't just list individual numbers.

## Conclusion

- **Separate summary from outlook.** First state what you did and what you found, then suggest directions for future research.
- **Don't introduce new results** here. The conclusion synthesizes, it does not analyze.
- Future research directions should be **concrete and actionable** — not *"further research is needed"* but *"extending the model to include time windows would allow..."*.
- **Don't overclaim**: use *"suggests"* rather than *"proves"*, *"indicates"* rather than *"demonstrates"* — unless you provide formal proof.


# Formatting Essentials

## Text Formatting (Markdown)

| What you want | What you write | Result |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Bold | `**bold text**` | **bold text** |
| Italic | `*italic text*` | *italic text* |
| Bold + italic | `***both***` | ***both*** |
| Inline code | `` `code` `` | `code` |
| Footnote | `text^[A footnote.]` | text^[A footnote.] |
| Link | `[text](https://...)` | [text](https://example.com) |
| En-dash (ranges) | `5--10` | 5--10 |

: Markdown text formatting {#tbl-formatting}

## Lists

:::::{.columns}
::::{.column width="48%"}
**Unordered list:**

```markdown
- Item 1
- Item 2
  - Nested item
```

- Item 1
- Item 2
  - Nested item
::::
::::{.column width="4%"}
::::
::::{.column width="48%"}
**Ordered list:**

```markdown
1. First
2. Second
   a. Sub-item
```

1. First
2. Second
   a. Sub-item
::::
:::::

## Citations

| Syntax | Output | Use when... |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `[@key]` | (Author, Year) | Supporting a claim at the end of a sentence |
| `@key` | Author (Year) | The author is the subject of the sentence |
| `[@a; @b]` | (Author1; Author2) | Citing multiple sources |
| `[@key, p. 42]` | (Author, Year, p. 42) | Pointing to a specific page |

: Citation syntax {#tbl-citations}

## Figures and Tables

**Figures** — include with a caption and label for cross-referencing:

```markdown
![Caption text](images/file.png){#fig-label width="80%"}
```

Reference in text with `@fig-label`.

**Tables** — use pipe syntax with a caption below the table:

```markdown
| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| Data 1   | Data 2   |

: Table caption {#tbl-label}
```

Reference in text with `@tbl-label`.

:::{.callout-important}
Every figure and table **must be referenced in the text before it appears**. Figures have captions below them; tables have captions above (in LaTeX) or below (in Quarto).
:::

## Equations

Write display equations with labels for cross-referencing:

```markdown
$$
\min \sum_{i} c_i \cdot x_i
$$ {#eq-objective}
```

$$
\min \sum_{i} c_i \cdot x_i
$$ {#eq-objective}

Reference in text with `@eq-objective`.

## Cross-References

| Element | Label syntax | Reference syntax |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Figure | `{#fig-name}` | `@fig-name` |
| Table | `{#tbl-name}` | `@tbl-name` |
| Equation | `{#eq-name}` | `@eq-name` |
| Section | `{#sec-name}` | `@sec-name` |

: Cross-reference syntax {#tbl-crossrefs}


# Common Mistakes

Avoid these frequent issues in seminar papers:

:::{.callout-warning}
## Checklist before submission

- [ ] Every figure and table is referenced in the text **before** it appears.
- [ ] Sentences never start with a math symbol. Write *"The variable $x$ is..."* instead of *"$x$ is..."*.
- [ ] Notation is consistent throughout — don't switch between $x_{ij}$ and $x(i,j)$.
- [ ] No orphan sections (a single subsection under a section).
- [ ] Each section is at least one page long. If not, merge it with another.
- [ ] Acronyms are defined on first use, then abbreviated consistently.
- [ ] No Wikipedia or lecture slides in the bibliography — find and cite the original source.
- [ ] En-dashes (`--`) are used for ranges (pages 5--10), not hyphens.
- [ ] Numbers one through twelve are spelled out in running text; 13 and above use digits.
- [ ] Units have a thin space before them: 16 GB, 245 s.
:::

## Table Design

- Use **booktabs style**: horizontal rules only (`\toprule`, `\midrule`, `\bottomrule`), no vertical lines.
- **Right-align** numeric columns, **left-align** text columns.
- Put **units in column headers**, not in every cell.
- Keep tables compact — remove unnecessary columns.


# Working with Typst

The Quarto templates use **Typst** (not LaTeX) to produce the PDF. For most writing, you only need Markdown. But for advanced formatting, you can embed raw Typst code:

```{{=typst}}
#v(1cm)                          // vertical space
#text(fill: red)[important]      // colored text
#align(center)[centered text]    // alignment
#line(length: 100%)              // horizontal line
#set text(size: 9pt)             // change font size locally
```

:::{.callout-note}
Typst is much simpler than LaTeX. See the [Typst documentation](https://typst.app/docs) for the full reference.
:::


# AI Tools Declaration

Both templates include a section for documenting your use of AI tools. This is **required** — fill it in honestly and specifically.

For each tool you used, describe:

- **Which tool** (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, GitHub Copilot, Grammarly)
- **For which tasks** (e.g., brainstorming structure, debugging code, proofreading)
- **How you verified the output** (e.g., checked against source material, tested code)

:::{.callout-important}
All AI-generated content must be reviewed, verified, and revised by you. You are responsible for the correctness of everything in your paper.
:::
